andrew-leigh-fenner.summary: Andrew Leigh is a strong advocate for free trade and unilateral tariff reduction, opposing protectionism. He has argued Australia should continue advocating for tariff removal without imposing retaliatory tariffs, stating "Australia is a trading nation and a strong advocate for open and rules-based trade" and "Trade expands opportunity. Isolation shrinks it."

sources.title: Leigh takes a swipe at US tariffs with passionate speech in defence of free trade

barnaby-joyce-new-england.summary: Barnaby Joyce has stated a preference for Australian ownership of agricultural assets, saying "My preference is always for the Australian people to be owner of the Australian asset" and "I'll never work to the exclusion of foreign investment but my preference is always for the Australian owner of the Australian asset." He supported blocking selected foreign bids (e.g. Kidman & Co) and warned against monopolistic foreign control of agricultural infrastructure.

sources.title: Barnaby Joyce's foreign investment odyssey

dai-le-fowler.summary: Dai Le has advocated for support of local small and medium manufacturing businesses, noting "Manufacturing is the largest employer in Fowler, standing at 40% yet the plan doesn't really invest in our local community, many of whom are small to medium sized manufacturing companies." She criticised the Future Made in Australia plan as inadequately supporting local manufacturers facing rising utility and input costs.

sources.title: Budget Reflections: A Missed Opportunity for Fowler and Australia

anthony-albanese-grayndler.summary: Anthony Albanese has championed the "Future Made in Australia" agenda, stating "Our Government is investing in manufacturing to make more things here" and that government must "be more assertive in capitalising on our comparative advantages and building sovereign capability in areas of national interest." He cited "a new and widespread willingness to make economic interventions on the basis of national interest and national sovereignty."

sources.title: A future made in Australia

ed-husic-chifley.summary: Ed Husic has advocated for rebuilding Australian domestic manufacturing and self-sufficiency, stating "Australia ranks last in a list of OECD countries in terms of manufacturing self-sufficiency" and "We want to send this signal to industry and its workers: Australian Labor is on the side of Australian manufacturing," describing manufacturing as "in the DNA of the Australian Labor Party."

sources.title: Manufacturing (speech)

alison-byrnes-cunningham.summary: In her first speech, Alison Byrnes voiced support for local steel manufacturing in the Illawarra, stating "We stand ready to use our skills to produce high quality steel in a more sustainable way," and emphasised local businesses and workers having the skills for the modern economy.

sources.title: My First Speech to Parliament

dan-repacholi-hunter.summary: In his maiden speech, Dan Repacholi backed the Hunter's coal industry and mining workforce, stating "coal will continue to play a role for many years to come in powering the world" and "I will make sure the Hunter remains at the forefront of supplying coal to the world," while also pointing to future local industries such as hydrogen, batteries, wind and solar.

sources.title: It is an exciting time for the Hunter electorate: Dan Repacholi's maiden speech to parliament

andrew-gee-calare.summary: In his maiden speech, Andrew Gee called for "a targeted, national manufacturing strategy," arguing "Other countries with high labour costs have been able to do it and we can too," and advocated value-adding to agriculture, noting "the vast majority of agricultural commodities produced in the central west leave the region without any value adding" and that food processing is a growth area for Australia.

sources.title: Andrew Gee (Nats-Calare) - Maiden Speech

alison-penfold-lyne.summary: In her maiden speech, Alison Penfold highlighted local farming, dairy, beef, oyster and timber industries as "valued economic drivers" of family-run small businesses in Lyne, backed "small business as the engine room of the economy," and defended the live export industry, criticising regulation she said "bans industries" and "adds costs to business."

sources.title: Ordinary Woman, Extraordinary Job: NSW Nationals Speech

alex-hawke-mitchell.summary: In a 2019 keynote, Alex Hawke referenced the Coalition government's legislation "establishing the kind of rules around foreign investment in critical national infrastructure" and "banning foreign donations into the Australian political system," stating this legislation "has really shored up the ability of Australia to stand strong" and that "a strong, secure sovereign region fundamentally enables economic prosperity."

sources.title: Australia's Focus on the Pacific - Hon Alex Hawke MP's Keynote Speech to the AIIA National Conference

chris-bowen-mcmahon.summary: Chris Bowen has backed bringing solar panel manufacturing to Australia under the Future Made in Australia agenda, stating "We invented the modern solar panel... We've been missing, by and large, the middle part of manufacturing solar panels," and that the Hunter "will be centre of our Future Made in Australia," linking the policy to local jobs and strengthened supply chains.

sources.title: Sunshot program backs "Sun King" to bring large-scale solar panel manufacturing back to Australia

fiona-phillips-gilmore.summary: Fiona Phillips criticised the federal Manufacturing Modernisation Fund for allocating only $100,000 to one company in her South Coast electorate, calling it "disgraceful" and "a drop in the ocean," and argued "the government needs to be providing more stimulus funds for the South Coast" to support local manufacturers.

sources.title: Phillips says it's disgraceful local manufacturers missed out on grants

kevin-hogan-page.summary: As Shadow Minister for Trade, Hogan called for the Government to give Australian producers and exporters impacted by US tariffs the same high-level advocacy shown in trade engagement with China, saying the Prime Minister must deliver trade outcomes for Australian exporters with all major partners.

sources.title: PM must bring same energy to US trade talks as China visit

pat-conroy-shortland.summary: As Minister for Defence Industry, Conroy stated that building a sovereign defence industry and ecosystem is at the heart of the Government's efforts and that this is the best way to ensure a defence future 'made in Australia, by Australians', adding the Government is committed to a defence future made in Australia to make the nation more prepared and resilient.

sources.title: Pat Conroy says defence future will be 'made in Australia' through manufacturing

mike-freelander-macarthur.summary: In his maiden speech, Freelander said he intends to make sure that in Macarthur 'we foster local jobs so that people can work locally'.

sources.title: Mike Freelander (ALP-Macarthur) - Maiden Speech

sharon-claydon-newcastle.summary: Speaking on the Future Made in Australia legislation, Claydon said 'we want Australians in secure jobs and we want Australians building things here' and that 'a future made in Australia means a future made in Newcastle', stating the Government is 'not interested in just simply shipping things overseas and then importing them back as more expensive finished products'.

sources.title: Future Made in Australia

sussan-ley-farrer.summary: As a Coalition minister, Ley promoted the Coalition Government's foreign investment regime to improve scrutiny and transparency around foreign ownership of Australia's agricultural production, noting the screening threshold for foreign purchases of agricultural land by private investors had been reduced to $15 million, and said sales of critical infrastructure to foreign investors must be properly scrutinised so that foreign investment is not contrary to the national interest.

sources.title: Stronger foreign investment regime comes into force

zali-steggall-warringah.summary: Speaking on the National Reconstruction Fund Corporation Bill, Steggall said 'it's incredibly important that the National Reconstruction Fund be used to support emerging technologies and key manufacturing sectors—not to prop up mature technologies, such as fossil fuel extraction'.

sources.title: National Reconstruction Fund Corporation Bill 2022, House debates

pat-conaghan-cowper.summary: Conaghan stated that 'collaboration is crucial if we are to build scale in Australian manufacturing and create the jobs we need now in the COVID-19 recovery and for generations to come', and said the aim is to support collaborations that spur private sector investment and help Australian manufacturers innovate and become more competitive.

sources.title: Government backs manufacturers to build scale and jobs

bob-katter-kennedy.summary: Katter holds protectionist and economic-nationalist views: he supports traditional Country Party statutory marketing for agriculture, opposes privatisation and economic deregulation, and has pioneered protests against imported produce (such as bananas) and championed mandating domestic ethanol fuel content. He resigned from the National Party in 2001 citing its abandonment of rural protectionist principles.

sources.title: Bob Katter

jim-chalmers-rankin.summary: As Treasurer, Chalmers announced the Future Made in Australia plan, framing it around building domestic sovereign capability to protect national security and economic resilience. He identified priority sectors critical to resilience and vulnerable to supply disruptions, backed value-adding to critical minerals, and announced support for battery and solar panel supply chains and sovereign defence industrial priorities.

sources.title: Investing in a Future Made in Australia

david-littleproud-maranoa.summary: Littleproud called on the Treasurer to revise parts of the Australia-US free trade agreement that allow US companies to buy Australian farming ventures worth up to $1.46 billion without Foreign Investment Review Board approval, a threshold not applied to other foreign investors. He said the current rules let cashed-up foreign buyers outcompete Australian farming families for farmland.

sources.title: Littleproud wants US trade deal revised amid American farm buy-up

andrew-willcox-dawson.summary: As Shadow Assistant Minister for Manufacturing and Sovereign Capability, Willcox said it is vital to drive the manufacturing industry forward, become more self-reliant and strengthen Australia's ability to 'stand on our own two feet as a nation', and that he would fight for policies that boost innovation, create long-term employment and restore confidence in Australia's industry.

sources.title: Member For Dawson Andrew Willcox Appointed Shadow Assistant Minister For Manufacturing And Sovereign Capabilities

angie-bell-moncrieff.summary: In a 2023 speech on the National Reconstruction Fund Corporation Bill, Bell said the Coalition's focus in government had been to modernise manufacturing, with an emphasis on sovereign capability, jobs, medical products and defence. She opposed Labor's National Reconstruction Fund, arguing manufacturers instead needed action on energy prices and labour shortages.

sources.title: National Reconstruction Fund Corporation Bill 2022

leon-rebello-mcpherson.summary: In his first speech, Rebello said Australia should support the expansion of its domestic manufacturing sector in line with its strategic and comparative advantage, and stated he is a firm believer in diversifying import and export markets so the country does not rely on any single country. He also called for an environment that welcomes foreign investment in the national interest.

sources.title: Maiden Speech

scott-buchholz-wright.summary: In his maiden speech, Buchholz said he would fight to get farmers a better price at the farm gate and emphasised agricultural self-reliance, warning that 'without these people we become captive to overseas producers and overseas markets; without these people we lose our dignity and our sovereignty.'

sources.title: Maiden Speech

llew-o-brien-wide-bay.summary: O'Brien said sustainable, world-class timber is produced in Wide Bay and that he is committed to ensuring the local timber industry remains viable, thrives and creates more jobs into the future, citing Hyne Timber as one of the region's major employers and government investment in the industry.

sources.title: Action needed to protect Wide Bay's timber industry

elizabeth-watson-brown-ryan.summary: In her first speech, Watson-Brown advocated a planned phase-out of coal and gas in favour of renewables and green manufacturing, and argued that public infrastructure should be in public hands, criticising the development and ownership of public infrastructure by private corporations.

sources.title: First Speech - Elizabeth Watson-Brown

terry-young-longman.summary: In his first speech (31 July 2019), Young emphasised local small business and local employment, describing Longman as made up of over 12,000 small businesses and praising local operators as "hardworking locals, employing locals and spending their money locally." He said "the onset of online businesses and multinationals has made the life of the local business owner that much more difficult" and pledged to break Longman's tradition of higher-than-average unemployment.

sources.title: Terry Young (Lib-Longman) - Maiden Speech

amanda-rishworth-kingston.summary: On her campaign page promoting "A Future Made in Australia," Rishworth's stated position is that she "wants to see more manufacturing and jobs in the South," linked to government infrastructure projects in South Australia she says will create local employment.

sources.title: A Future Made in Australia

matt-burnell-spence.summary: Burnell's official member profile states he "wants the Northern suburbs of Adelaide to be the hub of manufacturing once again, securing jobs for our future and generations to come," and lists fighting for "good, secure jobs" among his priorities.

sources.title: Matt Burnell | Member for Spence

rebekha-sharkie-mayo.summary: Under her "Primary Production" policy, Sharkie states that as SA's food bowl, Mayo requires "preservation of Australia's farmland and support for local grown produce and local manufacture."

sources.title: Where I Stand
sources.publisher: Rebekha Sharkie MP - Federal Member for Mayo

steve-georganas-adelaide.summary: Georganas's official member profile describes him as "a strong advocate for local jobs, fighting the offshoring of call centre work," and states he "supported a shift to onshore slaughter and meat processing to create more jobs."

sources.title: Steve Georganas | Member for Adelaide

tom-venning-grey.summary: On his official website, Venning states "Primary industries are the backbone of our economy. We provide the nation with minerals, food, fibre and energy," and says "As an economist and a 5th generation farmer, I know the importance of protecting these industries from inequitable taxes and unfair climate policy." He cites the farmer-paid biosecurity levy and the ban on live sheep exports as attacks on rural livelihoods.

sources.title: Tom Venning – Liberal Member for Grey

andrew-wilkie-clark.summary: On his "Where I stand" page, Wilkie states "Foreign investment needs much better regulation. Yes, Australia needs it, but only on our terms and only when it's genuinely in the national interest." He adds that "Assessment criteria must always include a national security test, and strategic assets including energy, transport infrastructure and broadacre prime agricultural land must never be sold or leased."

sources.title: Where I stand

anne-urquhart-braddon.summary: Urquhart's official member profile lists among her priorities "supporting well-paid jobs, strong local manufacturing," access to childcare and aged care, and education.

sources.title: Anne Urquhart | Member for Braddon

julie-collins-franklin.summary: As Minister for Agriculture, Fisheries and Forestry, Collins said the government is looking at "how do we do more manufacturing and more value-adding in terms of our supply chain, but importantly, of our raw product here in Australia," referencing the National Reconstruction Fund and supply-chain resilience.

sources.title: Julie Collins on reshaping Aussie agriculture

anne-webster-mallee.summary: Advocating local urea production, Webster said "in today's global climate, we need to be on the front foot with sovereign manufacturing capabilities" and "It's time for us to move away from foreign dependence on these and other essential products, and to become more self-reliant." She described investing in domestic urea production on "Aussie soil" as helping secure reliable fertiliser supply for growers.

sources.title: Growing Australia's Fertiliser Industry

basem-abdo-calwell.summary: Abdo's official member profile states that "Calwell, once a vibrant hub for manufacturing, has the potential to lead Australia's resurgence in this sector," that he "wants to see secure, well-paying jobs back in his community," and that "Australia's capacity to make things here starts by investing in the local workforce, fostering skills development, and providing strong support for industry growth."

sources.title: Basem Abdo | Member for Calwell

carina-garland-chisholm.summary: Garland's profile states she "is passionate about bringing manufacturing back to Australia and is fighting for opportunities for local manufacturers," wants "a Future Made in Australia, where our nation capitalises on our natural resources to become a renewable energy superpower," and is working to build "secure local jobs."

sources.title: Carina Garland | Labor Federal Team

clare-o-neil-hotham.summary: In her 2013 first speech, O'Neil said government "should not be building great tariff walls or controlling the big macroeconomic levers," arguing instead that government supports business competitiveness through a skilled population, world-class infrastructure, safe cities and a culture of invention. She referenced modern manufacturing (the Corning NBN-fibre factory) as "made possible by a clever and adaptable workforce."

sources.title: Clare O'Neil (ALP - Hotham) - First Speech

dan-tehan-wannon.summary: As Minister for Trade, Tourism and Investment, Tehan took over and concluded the Australia-United Kingdom Free Trade Agreement, having earlier been involved in negotiating the Australia-United States Free Trade Agreement in 2004. His Wikipedia biography records his role advancing trade liberalisation agreements.

sources.title: Dan Tehan

darren-chester-gippsland.summary: Announcing a $3 million grant to DefendTex in Gippsland, Chester supported domestically producing commercial rocket boosters to fill a gap where there are "no commercially available, domestically manufactured rocket boosters for launch in Australia," saying this would "allow for responsive sovereign space launch to occur" and grow local jobs in Gippsland.

sources.title: $3 Million To Advance Manufacturing In Gippsland

ged-kearney-cooper.summary: In her first speech, Kearney argued that money from corporate tax cuts could instead "build skills, support innovative projects and target and fund growth strategies for high-wage, high-skill industries, like niche manufacturing, science and technology, logistics, education, health and social services."

sources.title: SPEECH (House of Representatives): First Speech to Parliament

helen-haines-indi.summary: Launching her plan for community-owned renewable energy in regional Australia, Haines advocated that "everyday people develop, build or benefit from an energy project," saying that doing renewables right would mean "lower bills, stronger energy security, and new job and industries" and a "regional jobs boom" if every school, council, hospital, sporting club and community group could access the benefits.

sources.title: Helen Haines launches renewable energy plan for regional Australia

julian-hill-bruce.summary: In a 2023 statement titled "Australia must make more things here," Hill said "We can make more things here. We should make more things here and we must make more things here," warning Australia is "around the lowest for manufacturing self-sufficiency of any developed country in the world" and "too vulnerable to supply shocks" and "to military blockades." He called for urgently boosting "sovereign manufacturing capability" while acknowledging "we can't make everything here."

sources.title: Australia must make more things here

lisa-chesters-bendigo.summary: In her 2013 first speech, Chesters highlighted Bendigo manufacturers (Australian Turntables, Industrial Conveying Australia, Keech Casting and Hofmann Engineering) as "world leaders in their fields exporting to countries all over the world," and said it was "ideologically driven policies of governments" rather than red tape that created roadblocks hindering their growth, criticising policies she said blocked Hofmann from expanding into wind energy generation.

sources.title: First speech for new member

libby-coker-corangamite.summary: In her 2019 first speech, Coker said "We shouldn't stand idly by and watch high-skilled, well-paid manufacturing and public sector jobs disappear while the market replaces them with low-paid, low-skill insecure work," and called for "sector plans, like the famous Button car and steel plants, for each targeted industry" plus investment in research, technology, start-ups and value-adding industries.

sources.title: Elizabeth Coker (ALP-Corangamite) - Maiden Speech

mary-aldred-monash.summary: As candidate/Member for Monash, Aldred said "We need to protect our industries. If the timber industry dies, that will have a devastating impact on local schools, on local businesses, on local community spirit," and pledged to "look after small businesses" and to "fight very hard for all our farmers to continue to have the right to farming."

sources.title: Meet Mary Aldred; Federal Liberal candidate for Monash

richard-marles-corio.summary: Outlining ALP defence policy as Shadow Defence Minister, Marles said "The ability to sustain and maintain the equipment the ADF uses is fundamental to our national security and it is essential that we always retain the industrial capability to meet this objective," that "At its best, Australian industry is as capable as any in the world," and advocated that "an Australian shipbuilder be mandated for all future shipbuilding programs."

sources.title: Shadow Defence Minister Richard Marles outlines ALP Defence policy

tim-wilson-goldstein.summary: In his 2016 maiden speech, Tim Wilson championed open markets and free trade, saying his predecessor's legacy was "to open markets" and that "the benefits of trade agreements begin with the signing of a pen but finish with new opportunity." He framed Australia as a net capital importer needing a competitive tax system to attract foreign investment, and advocated restructuring domestic sectors into "outward-looking export industries" rather than protecting local industry through tariffs.

sources.title: Tim Wilson (Lib-Goldstein) - Maiden Speech

andrew-hastie-canning.summary: Andrew Hastie, Shadow Minister for Industry and Sovereign Capability, has argued for rebuilding Australian manufacturing as a matter of sovereignty, stating "Our need for a reborn Australian manufacturing sector is now vital. Our sovereignty depends upon it, our allies and partners depend upon it." He defined self-reliance as "the ability of Australia to domestically manufacture the goods and services we need," said "if globalisation led to the offshoring of Australian manufacturing, then the new geopolitical disorder can bring it back home," and called for governments to support and "pick winners" in industry.

sources.title: Hastie speech signals Opposition emphasis on national resilience

josh-wilson-fremantle.summary: Josh Wilson has advocated for Australian shipbuilding and sovereign industrial capacity, saying that "as an island continent nation, we need to have that sovereign capacity, both in terms of the industrial side of it and the workforce and skills side of it." He said he is glad to be part of a government delivering an Australian strategic fleet because the nation "can't go without that capacity," referencing the shipbuilding hub at Henderson in his electorate.

sources.title: Josh Wilson Responds To Lack Of Independence Claims

madeleine-king-brand.summary: As Minister for Resources, Madeleine King announced a Critical Minerals Strategic Reserve, stating "while we will continue to supply the world with critical minerals, it is also important that Australia has access to the critical minerals and rare earths we need for a Future Made in Australia," and noting the initiative would generate additional investment and employment, particularly in Western Australia.

sources.title: Australia to establish critical minerals strategic reserve to boost economic resilience

rick-wilson-o-connor.summary: In his 2013 first speech, Rick Wilson said he stands "proudly for the Liberal philosophy of free markets, vigorous competition, small government and individual responsibility." He cited foreign investment positively, noting that "it was syndicated American money that was behind the development of the sand plain into an extraordinarily productive farming region" around Esperance, and welcomed the rebuilding of the live animal export trade.

sources.title: Rick Wilson (Lib-O'Connor) - First Speech

sam-lim-tangney.summary: Sam Lim has backed local manufacturing and local jobs in connection with defence industry grants, stating "by backing local manufacturing, the Albanese Labor Government is investing in high-skilled, local jobs right here in Tangney," and framing the support as building Australia's local capability and employment.

sources.title: Strengthening Tangney's Defence Industry

zaneta-mascarenhas-swan.summary: In her first speech, Zaneta Mascarenhas argued "in WA we need to build beyond the resources boom and make our economy more resilient," and that "Australia needs to increase diversification, commercialisation and sophistication of our industry capability." She said Australia is rich in critical minerals to "build batteries, wind turbines and solar panels" and could become a "renewable energy superpower," advocating use of the National Reconstruction Fund to build advanced manufacturing capability, including the cell-manufacturing "value-add step."

sources.title: Proud moment for Indian-Australians: First Speech - Ms Zaneta Mascarenhas MP

david-pocock-act.summary: Independent Senator David Pocock has advocated reforming government procurement to favour Australian companies and small businesses, saying Australia should be "investing in Australian companies - sovereign Australian companies - that are building up their capability in a whole range of industries from defence to quantum computing... and not just going with multinationals." He criticised multinationals "skimming the cream off the top and subcontracting that to smaller Australian companies" as "a ridiculous use of taxpayer money," and called for a system where smaller Australian companies can win contracts.

sources.title: Small businesses deserve a fair shot at winning government procurement contracts says Senator Pocock

andrew-bragg-nsw.summary: In his 2019 maiden speech, Andrew Bragg argued against protectionism, saying "this nation will never have enough domestic capital to meet our high ambitions" and that "the argument that we should close down foreign investment is akin to raising the tariff walls." He said "almost one-quarter of our economy is exports... we simply cannot afford to take protectionist risks," while adding Australia "must maintain a liberal approach to trade and foreign investment whilst ensuring we do all the appropriate due diligence, and reserve the right to legislate to protect Australia's security, economic and social interests."

sources.title: Sen. Andrew Bragg (Lib-NSW) - Maiden Speech

tim-ayres-nsw.summary: Ayres states in his first speech that he advocates for strong manufacturing industries and good blue-collar jobs, especially in Australia's regions, noting Australia lost 25 per cent of its manufacturing workforce between 2012 and 2016, and he criticises the offshoring of jobs and concentration of economic activity in big cities.

sources.title: About Tim Ayres

malcolm-roberts-qld.summary: In his 2016 maiden speech, Roberts stated that '90 per cent of Australia's large companies are foreign owned', that 'the people of Australia are desperate to regain our sovereignty' and 'Australians have had enough of foreign control', and criticised de-industrialisation policies as 'costing jobs, destroying families, bankrupting businesses'.

sources.title: Sen. Malcolm Roberts (One Nation-Qld) Maiden Speech

matthew-canavan-qld.summary: In a January 2021 policy paper, Canavan called to reverse the decline in Australian manufacturing by reinvesting in coal and gas, 'protecting Australian jobs from unfair overseas competition', introducing a 'Buy Australian Act' to give preference to Australian-made goods in government contracts, and protecting key manufacturing industries including by tariffs; he wrote that this challenges 'longstanding commitments to free trade and the removal of industry protection' but that security challenges require backing Australian industrial development.

sources.title: It's Time to Manufacture Jobs

pauline-hanson-qld.summary: In a May 2026 interview, Hanson said 'I don't believe foreigners should own any housing in Australia or our farming land', and proposed giving foreign owners two years to sell or have property repossessed by the government (later clarifying this would apply to temporary visa holders and foreigners living overseas, not permanent residents). In a December 2020 media release she also opposed 'predatory foreign investment' and foreign takeovers of Australian businesses across sectors including manufacturing and mining.

sources.title: One Nation leader Pauline Hanson threatened to take control of foreign-owned farms
Australians force Govt action against predatory foreign ownership

sean-bell-nsw.summary: In his 26 November 2025 maiden speech, Bell said Australian manufacturing 'is being shut down' and that industry such as Tomago Aluminium is 'on the brink', that coalmining jobs sustaining regions like the Hunter 'are being sacrificed', and criticised reliance on 'Chinese-made solar panels and foreign supply chains' as 'national self-harm', adding that a young family 'should not have to compete with every millionaire from China, India or the United States' to buy a home.

sources.title: Bell, Senator Sean: 26 Nov 2025: Senate debates
Sean Bell: we are not here to apologise for Australia, we are here to defend it

jacqui-lambie-tas.summary: Through her "Make Australia Make Again" (MAMA) campaign, Lambie calls for reviving domestic manufacturing, arguing "What we make, we control" and that government "has an obligation to identify the core industrial capabilities Australia needs." She cites the Whyalla steelworks as a "critical" sovereign capability, advocates putting "the brakes on foreign purchases of our critical assets," and promotes a "Back local. Back Australia" approach of investing in Australian industry rather than competitors.

sources.title: MAMA Make Australia Make Again

bridget-mckenzie-vic.summary: McKenzie supported reducing the foreign investment screening threshold to zero dollars so that all proposed foreign investments would be scrutinised, citing concern about "opportunistic foreign raiders getting control of Australia's food supply assets" and stating "it is not in our national interest to leave the door open wide for foreign opportunists to take advantage of our clean, green reputation and assets." She framed the measures as protecting Australian agricultural land.

sources.title: Foreign investment change protects Aussie farm land

peter-whish-wilson-tas.summary: Whish-Wilson established a Senate inquiry into foreign investment, stating that "the government's failure to ensure the integrity of the foreign investment approval process is undermining what faith the public has that these takeovers are being approved in the national interest." He raised concerns about foreign takeovers (citing Bellamy's and VDL Dairy) and potential weaknesses, including money-laundering vulnerabilities, in Australia's foreign investment and property-purchase framework.

sources.title: Tasmanian Greens senator Peter Whish-Wilson establishes Senate inquiry into foreign investment

richard-colbeck-tas.summary: Colbeck called for a Senate inquiry into the Australian metals manufacturing industry, stating "these assets need to be supported and protected, because... if we lose these assets, it will be nearly impossible to recover them," and that "a 'Future Made in Australia' needs to be more than just a catchphrase" with government supporting existing facilities. He has also advocated for protecting jobs in Tasmanian salmon, forestry and other regional industries.

sources.title: Press releases

raff-ciccone-vic.summary: On his official website, Ciccone advocates for Australia building domestic industrial and manufacturing capacity, stating: "Australia must be a country that makes things, to have our own industrial and manufacturing capabilities," and arguing that COVID showed "the need for Australia to be a place which makes things... our own sovereign capabilities." He supports the National Reconstruction Fund to diversify Australia's industrial base and develop national sovereign capability.

sources.title: A future made in Australia

ralph-babet-vic.summary: Per a profile of Babet drawing on his August 2022 first speech, he emphasises the importance of Australian superannuation funds being invested locally to boost the domestic economy.

sources.title: Ralph Babet | Parliamentary Leader of the United Australia Party

michelle-ananda-rajah-vic.summary: In her first speech and related remarks, Ananda-Rajah argues Australia should build sovereign capability in biotechnology, bridging the "missing middle" so domestic biotech firms can achieve scale onshore rather than commercialising overseas, and positions Australia as a global clinical trials destination. She frames science as a pillar of national economic strategy with sovereign capability "now a national priority."

sources.title: Senator Ananda-Rajah's Science and Sovereignty Vision

sarah-henderson-vic.summary: In her maiden Senate speech (16 October 2019), Henderson stated that Australia's critical infrastructure assets "such as airports, power stations, data networks, communications infrastructure and ports - including the Port of Darwin - should simply not be falling into foreign hands," and called for foreign investment laws to be re-examined to limit foreign acquisition of prime agricultural land.

sources.title: Sarah Henderson - Wikipedia

ellie-whiteaker-wa.summary: According to her Wikipedia article, Whiteaker's inaugural Senate speech (23 July 2025) highlighted support for policies promoting public sector investment in health care and manufacturing, alongside workers' rights and gender equality. She is affiliated with the Australian Manufacturing Workers' Union.

sources.title: Ellie Whiteaker - Wikipedia
